name: Suicidal Ideation
summary: Suicidal thoughts with or without plan/intent/means; differentiate passive ideation from active plan with imminent risk.

triage: Bring directly back; do not leave in open waiting room
Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) or PHQ-9 screen
Ask directly about plan, intent, access to means, prior attempts
Assess for intoxication/coingestion as contributing factor
Flag for 1:1 continuous observation
nursing: Initiate 1:1 sitter / continuous line-of-sight observation
Search patient and belongings; remove sharps, cords, belts, medications, lighters
Place in safe room (ligature-resistant) in patient gown
Document mood, affect, and statements verbatim
Secure personal effects and any brought-in medications
keyOrders: IV access* if indicated (suspected coingestion/medical workup)
Capture and lock up any weapons/medications brought by patient
Notify behavioral health / on-call psychiatry early
labs: Beta-hCG in persons of childbearing potential
imaging: CT head* if indicated for trauma, altered mental status, or focal deficit
meds: Treat agitation only if needed: lorazepam 1-2 mg PO/IM/IV
Haloperidol 5 mg IM + lorazepam 2 mg IM* if indicated for severe agitation
Continue home psychiatric meds if clinically appropriate
Avoid PRN opioids/sedatives that may worsen disinhibition
reassess: Repeat suicide risk assessment after intoxication clears
Reassess intent, plan, protective factors, and support system
Document collateral from family/EMS/outpatient providers
Stratify risk: low (passive) vs high (active plan/intent/means)
disposition: Low risk with safety plan and support: discharge to outpatient follow-up
Moderate-high risk: psychiatric evaluation and inpatient admission
FSED has no psych beds: arrange appropriate transfer to psychiatric facility
Consider emergency detention (peace-officer or physician application) if refusing and high risk
discharge: Only if cleared by formal risk assessment and intoxication resolved
Provide written safety plan and crisis resources (988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline)
Means restriction counseling with family/caregiver
Ensure responsible adult escort and lethal-means removal at home
Document capacity and shared decision-making
followup: Primary care or behavioral health warm handoff
Crisis line and local mental health authority contact info
Return precautions for worsening thoughts or new plan

redFlags: Active plan with intent and access to lethal means
Recent attempt or rehearsal behavior
Command hallucinations to self-harm
Refusal to engage with inability to contract for safety
Coingestion or unstable vitals requiring medical stabilization
compliance: EMTALA: perform medical screening exam to rule out organic cause before psych disposition; stabilize coingestion/medical emergency first
FSED with no psychiatric capability must arrange appropriate transfer with accepting facility, qualified personnel, and records per EMTALA
Document observation-vs-transfer decision and continuous monitoring
Texas emergency detention: physician may execute application for emergency detention or notify peace officer if patient is a danger and refuses voluntary care (Texas Health & Safety Code Ch. 573)
Report suspected abuse/neglect if disclosed during evaluation

name: Homicidal Ideation
summary: Thoughts/threats of harming others; assess plan, intent, identifiable target, means, and underlying psychosis or intoxication.

triage: Bring to secure area; alert staff and security immediately
Assess for plan, identifiable target, intent, and access to weapons
Screen for intoxication, psychosis, and prior violence
Limit access to objects usable as weapons
Flag for continuous observation and de-escalation
nursing: Initiate 1:1 observation with security present
Search and remove weapons, sharps, and dangerous items
Patient in gown in safe/ligature-resistant room
Document statements verbatim and named targets
Maintain de-escalating, low-stimulation environment
keyOrders: IV access* if indicated for medical/sedation needs
Notify facility risk management / legal re: duty to warn
imaging: CT head* if indicated for trauma, AMS, or focal neuro findings
meds: Lorazepam 1-2 mg PO/IM/IV for agitation/anxiety
Haloperidol 5 mg IM + lorazepam 2 mg IM* if indicated for severe agitation
Olanzapine 10 mg IM or ziprasidone 10-20 mg IM as alternatives
Continue home antipsychotics if appropriate
reassess: Reassess after intoxication/psychosis treated
Re-evaluate intent, plan, and identifiability of target
Obtain collateral history
Stratify acute violence risk
disposition: Credible threat to identifiable victim: psychiatric admission and likely law-enforcement notification
FSED without psych beds: appropriate transfer to psychiatric/inpatient facility
Emergency detention if dangerous and refusing care
Low risk with no plan/intent: outpatient referral with documentation
discharge: Only after formal risk assessment clears patient
Document absence of plan, intent, and means
Provide crisis and behavioral health resources
Ensure safe environment and support; return precautions
followup: Local mental health authority / crisis services
Anger management or counseling referral as appropriate

redFlags: Specific plan with identifiable intended victim and means
Active psychosis with command hallucinations to harm others
History of violence with current intent
Access to firearms or weapons
Escalating agitation despite de-escalation
compliance: EMTALA: medical screening exam to exclude organic cause; stabilize before disposition

Texas Tarasoff-equivalent: limited duty to warn/protect — may disclose to medical/law enforcement and the identifiable potential victim when patient makes a serious threat of imminent physical harm (Texas Health & Safety Code 611.004)
Document notification of intended victim and/or law enforcement
FSED without psychiatric capability must arrange appropriate transfer with accepting facility per EMTALA
Texas emergency detention may apply if patient is dangerous and refuses voluntary admission (HSC Ch. 573)

name: Acute Agitation / Psychosis
summary: Severe behavioral disturbance; must exclude organic causes (hypoxia, hypoglycemia, sepsis, intoxication, head injury) before assuming primary psychiatric cause.

triage: Rapid safety assessment; call security and additional staff
Vital signs including pulse oximetry and temperature
Fingerstick glucose immediately
Attempt verbal de-escalation first
Clear room of hazards; ensure staff exit path
nursing: 1:1 observation; prepare physical restraints only if least-restrictive measures fail
Apply cardiac monitor and continuous pulse oximetry once safe
Establish IV access when feasible
Document restraint indication, type, and frequent neurovascular checks
Search for weapons and remove hazards
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, frequent vitals
Restraint order with time limits per protocol* if indicated
labs: Beta-hCG in persons of childbearing potential
imaging: CT head* if indicated for trauma, focal deficit, new-onset psychosis, or persistent AMS
meds: Midazolam 5 mg IM or 2-5 mg IV for rapid control
Haloperidol 5-10 mg IM + lorazepam 2 mg IM (combo) for severe agitation
Olanzapine 10 mg IM (avoid combining IM with parenteral benzo) or ziprasidone 10-20 mg IM
Ketamine 4-5 mg/kg IM* if indicated for severe/excited-delirium agitation (monitor airway)
Diphenhydramine 25-50 mg + benztropine for EPS/dystonia* if indicated
reassess: Continuous monitoring of airway, sedation level, SpO2, vitals
Reassess every 15 min during restraint/sedation; release restraints as soon as safe
Recheck glucose and treat reversible causes
Repeat ECG/QTc after antipsychotics
Watch for over-sedation and respiratory depression
disposition: Identify and treat organic cause if found (medical admission/transfer)
Primary psychiatric cause stabilized: psychiatric evaluation/admission
FSED without capability: appropriate transfer once medically stabilized
ICU-level transfer if airway compromise or refractory agitation
discharge: Only if fully sober, organically cleared, and risk-assessed
Confirm return to baseline mental status
Responsible adult escort and follow-up arranged
Provide medication and return precautions
followup: Outpatient or community mental health linkage

redFlags: Hypoxia, hypoglycemia, hyperthermia, or unstable vitals (organic emergency)
Excited delirium with extreme agitation, hyperthermia, and metabolic acidosis
New-onset psychosis with focal neurologic findings
Respiratory depression or over-sedation after chemical restraint
Rhabdomyolysis or QTc prolongation
compliance: EMTALA: agitation is a medical emergency — perform MSE and exclude/stabilize organic causes before psychiatric disposition
Restraint/seclusion documentation per CMS/Texas rules: least-restrictive means, physician order, time-limited, frequent monitoring
FSED must stabilize then arrange appropriate transfer (accepting facility, qualified personnel) if no inpatient/psych capability
Document chemical-restraint rationale and continuous monitoring
Report suspected abuse/neglect or assault if disclosed

name: Anxiety / Panic Attack
summary: Acute anxiety/panic with somatic symptoms; must exclude ACS, PE, arrhythmia, hypoglycemia, hyperthyroidism, and intoxication/withdrawal.

triage: Vital signs including SpO2 and temperature
Screen for chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations to risk-stratify cardiac/PE
Calm, low-stimulation environment; reassurance
Assess for substance use, caffeine, stimulants, or withdrawal
Screen for suicidal ideation
nursing: Place on monitor if cardiopulmonary symptoms
Coach paced breathing; avoid paper-bag rebreathing
Quiet room and supportive presence
Reassess vitals after de-escalation
Document mental status and triggers
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor* if indicated for tachyarrhythmia
labs: Troponin* if indicated for chest pain/cardiac risk
D-dimer* if indicated when PE suspected (per Wells/PERC)
imaging: CXR* if indicated for dyspnea/abnormal exam
CT pulmonary angiogram* if indicated for PE risk after risk stratification
meds: Lorazepam 0.5-1 mg PO/IV for acute severe symptoms (limit/avoid in substance-use risk)
Hydroxyzine 25-50 mg PO as non-benzo option
Continue/start SSRI as outpatient bridge (not ED-initiated acutely)
Treat underlying cause if identified
reassess: Reassess symptoms and vitals after intervention
Confirm resolution of tachycardia/tachypnea
Re-screen for SI and capacity for outpatient care
Confirm no missed organic diagnosis
disposition: Most discharge home after symptom resolution and reassurance
Admit/transfer if cardiac, PE, or other organic emergency identified
Refer to behavioral health for recurrent panic disorder
Reassess disposition if comorbid SI present
discharge: Reassurance and education on panic/anxiety physiology
Breathing and grounding techniques handout
Limit caffeine/stimulants; avoid alcohol
Short-term anxiolytic only if appropriate; counsel dependence risk
Return precautions for chest pain, syncope, dyspnea
followup: Psychiatry referral for recurrent panic disorder

redFlags: Chest pain, ECG changes, or elevated troponin (ACS)
Hypoxia or pleuritic pain suggesting PE
Syncope or sustained tachyarrhythmia
Signs of hyperthyroid storm or stimulant toxicity
Concurrent suicidal ideation
compliance: EMTALA: complete MSE to exclude life-threatening cardiopulmonary causes before attributing to anxiety
Document negative cardiac/PE workup or risk-stratification rationale
FSED should arrange appropriate transfer if organic emergency requires admission
Screen and document suicide risk before discharge
Counsel benzodiazepine dependence/diversion risk if prescribed

name: Substance Intoxication
summary: Acute intoxication from alcohol/stimulants/opioids/sedatives/hallucinogens; protect airway, exclude trauma and coingestion, and ensure safe sobering.

triage: Vital signs, SpO2, temperature, fingerstick glucose
Assess airway, breathing, and level of consciousness
Identify substance(s), time, and route if possible
Evaluate for trauma and concurrent medical illness
Screen for SI/HI once able to participate
nursing: Continuous observation; position to protect airway (recovery position)
Cardiac monitor and continuous pulse oximetry
IV access and fall precautions
Frequent reorientation and reassessment
Search and secure belongings/substances
keyOrders: Naloxone at bedside* if indicated for opioid component
labs: Acetaminophen and salicylate levels (rule out coingestion)
Serum osmolality / osm gap* if indicated (toxic alcohols)
Beta-hCG in persons of childbearing potential
imaging: CT head* if indicated for trauma, AMS out of proportion, or focal deficit
meds: Naloxone 0.04-0.4 mg IV titrated (or 2-4 mg IN) for opioid intoxication/respiratory depression
Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV for stimulant-related agitation/seizure
IV isotonic fluids for dehydration
Thiamine 100 mg IV before glucose if alcohol/malnutrition suspected
Antiemetic (ondansetron 4 mg IV)* if indicated
reassess: Serial neuro and respiratory assessments until clinically sober
Trend mental status toward baseline
Re-screen for SI/HI and capacity when sober
Watch for sedative re-narcotization (e.g., long-acting opioids)
disposition: Observe until clinically sober, ambulatory, and tolerating PO
Admit/transfer if airway risk, persistent AMS, or serious coingestion
Psychiatric evaluation if SI/HI persists after sobering
Refer to substance use treatment
discharge: Only when clinically sober with stable vitals and intact gait/judgment
Confirm no SI/HI and decision-making capacity
Responsible adult escort; no driving
Provide substance use resources and naloxone kit if opioid-related
Return precautions for recurrence/decompensation
followup: Substance use disorder / addiction medicine referral
Behavioral health if comorbid psychiatric illness

redFlags: Respiratory depression or airway compromise
Persistent or worsening altered mental status
Hyperthermia, seizures, or unstable arrhythmia (stimulants/serotonin syndrome)
Suspected toxic alcohol or large coingestion
Trauma with intoxication masking injury
compliance: EMTALA: MSE and stabilization; intoxicated patients lack capacity to refuse — cannot discharge until clinically sober and safe
Document capacity assessment and clinical sobriety prior to discharge
FSED must arrange appropriate transfer for ICU/medical admission needs (airway, severe toxicity)
Offer naloxone and harm reduction; report per public health requirements where applicable
Screen for SI/HI and abuse/trafficking indicators; report suspected abuse as mandated

name: Overdose / Poisoning
summary: Intentional or accidental ingestion; identify agent and timing, support ABCs, give antidotes, and consult Poison Control early.

triage: Immediate ABC assessment; vitals, SpO2, temperature, glucose
Identify substance(s), amount, time, and intent
Call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) early
Continuous observation; assume worsening course
Bring pill bottles/containers for identification
nursing: Cardiac monitor, continuous pulse oximetry, frequent vitals
Two large-bore IVs
Prepare airway equipment and antidotes
1:1 observation (intentional ingestion = self-harm risk)
Document time of ingestion and interventions
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, frequent neuro checks
Supplemental O2 / airway management as needed
IV access x2 with isotonic fluids
Activated charcoal* if indicated within ~1 hr, airway protected
labs: Acetaminophen and salicylate levels (mandatory in all intentional ODs)
Serum osmolality and osm gap (toxic alcohols)
Specific drug levels (digoxin, lithium, valproate, etc.)* if indicated
Beta-hCG in persons of childbearing potential
imaging: CXR* if indicated for aspiration/pulmonary edema
CT head* if indicated for AMS/trauma
Abdominal XR* if indicated for radiopaque tablets/body packing
meds: N-acetylcysteine per protocol for acetaminophen toxicity (150 mg/kg load)
Naloxone 0.04-2 mg IV titrated for opioid toxicity
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV bolus for wide-QRS TCA toxicity
Flumazenil generally avoided (seizure risk); benzos for stimulant/sympathomimetic toxicity
Agent-specific antidotes per Poison Control (e.g., glucagon, calcium, fomepizole, lipid emulsion)
reassess: Serial vitals, mental status, ECG, and respiratory status
Trend repeat acetaminophen/salicylate levels and labs
Anticipate delayed toxicity (sustained-release, late-peaking agents)
Continuous re-evaluation per Poison Control guidance
Reassess psychiatric risk once medically stabilized
disposition: Most intentional ODs require admission/observation and psychiatric evaluation
ICU transfer for hemodynamic instability, airway, dysrhythmia, or antidote infusions
FSED: stabilize and arrange appropriate higher-level transfer
Discharge only for low-risk minimal ingestion after adequate observation and psych clearance
discharge: Rare; only after toxicologic and psychiatric clearance
Document Poison Control concurrence and observation window completed
Means restriction and safety planning
Responsible adult escort and follow-up
Detailed return precautions
followup: Substance use treatment referral if applicable

redFlags: Airway compromise, respiratory depression, or hypoventilation
Wide-complex dysrhythmia or hemodynamic instability
Severe acidosis or rising acetaminophen/salicylate levels
Seizures or rapidly declining mental status
Co-ingestant or delayed-release agent with delayed toxicity
compliance: EMTALA: high-acuity MSE and aggressive stabilization before any transfer
FSED must arrange appropriate transfer to facility with ICU/toxicology/psychiatric capability when stabilization exceeds resources
Document Poison Control consultation and stabilizing care provided
Intentional overdose triggers continuous observation and suicide risk protocol; consider Texas emergency detention if high-risk and refusing
Mandatory reporting: suspected abuse/neglect, sexual assault, or child/elder endangerment if disclosed

name: Alcohol Withdrawal
summary: Spectrum from tremor/anxiety to withdrawal seizures and delirium tremens; symptom-triggered benzodiazepines and risk stratification are key.

triage: Vital signs, temperature, fingerstick glucose
Quantify last drink, intake pattern, prior withdrawal/DTs/seizures
Initiate CIWA-Ar scoring
Assess for tremor, diaphoresis, agitation, hallucinations
Screen for concurrent trauma/infection
nursing: Continuous monitoring; fall and seizure precautions
Serial CIWA-Ar assessments
Cardiac monitor and IV access
Quiet, low-stimulation environment; frequent reorientation
Strict intake/output and neuro checks
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, frequent vitals
labs: Acetaminophen/salicylate levels* if indicated for co-ingestion
Lipase* if indicated for abdominal pain
imaging: CT head* if indicated for seizure, trauma, or focal deficit
CXR* if indicated for suspected pneumonia/aspiration
meds: Lorazepam 2-4 mg IV symptom-triggered (preferred in hepatic impairment) per CIWA
Diazepam 5-10 mg IV q5-10 min for severe symptoms (front-loading)
Phenobarbital* if indicated for benzo-refractory withdrawal
Thiamine 100-500 mg IV before glucose; folate and multivitamin
Replete magnesium 2 g IV and correct electrolytes
Dexmedetomidine/propofol* if indicated for refractory DTs (ICU)
reassess: Repeat CIWA-Ar and titrate benzodiazepines to symptom control
Monitor for escalating agitation, hallucinations, or autonomic instability
Watch for over-sedation/respiratory depression
Reassess electrolytes and hydration
Identify progression toward delirium tremens
disposition: Mild (CIWA <8-10) with good support: discharge with outpatient detox referral
Moderate-severe or escalating CIWA: admission for symptom-triggered therapy
Delirium tremens / refractory withdrawal: ICU-level care — transfer from FSED
Withdrawal seizure or comorbid illness: admit/transfer
discharge: Only for mild, controlled symptoms with reliable follow-up and support
Thiamine and multivitamin prescription
Counsel relapse and withdrawal-recurrence risks
Provide outpatient detox/addiction resources
Return precautions for seizures, confusion, fever, worsening tremor
followup: Primary care within days for electrolyte and clinical recheck
Behavioral health for alcohol use disorder

redFlags: Delirium tremens: confusion, severe autonomic instability, hallucinations
Withdrawal seizures (especially repeated or status)
Benzodiazepine-refractory agitation
Hyperthermia, severe tachycardia, or hemodynamic instability
Concurrent infection, pancreatitis, GI bleed, or head injury
compliance: EMTALA: MSE and stabilization; exclude concurrent emergencies (trauma, sepsis, GI bleed)
FSED must arrange appropriate transfer to ICU-capable facility for DTs or refractory withdrawal
Document CIWA-driven treatment, observation-vs-transfer decision, and capacity
Administer thiamine before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy and document
Mandatory reporting: suspected abuse/neglect or impaired-driving concerns where applicable; offer SUD resources
